narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there with your middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time. The admission model we use predicted a 64.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure students are set up for long-term success. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the lowest group compared to typical students who average around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom group well below average, added to this concern by signaling early academic struggles that often carry over. Your LSAT score, lower than the strong averages seen in successful applicants, further nudged the prediction toward failure risk. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below average, helped pull the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your college performance. Your middle family income bracket worked in your favor too, as it aligns with many who succeed despite not coming from wealthier backgrounds. Being full-time, like most students, provided a small boost since it matches the typical path to better outcomes. Overall, your profile stands out as riskier than average students because your lower deciles and LSAT lag behind the stronger academic markers that usually predict bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and consider programs or tutoring proven to support students from backgrounds like yours. You're capable of turning this around, and we encourage reapplying after strengthening these areas. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
